key_concepts: The evolution from convolutional feature maps to treating images as sequences, popularized by Vision Transformers (ViT).
Image patches: breaking an image down into a grid of non-overlapping smaller squares (patches), flattening them, and treating them sequentially—much like words in a sentence.

Image embeddings: compressing the entire semantic content of a visual scene into a single, dense, continuous vector. In this vector space, visually and semantically similar images are located close together.
This paradigm shift unifies the representation format across modalities, allowing image patches to be processed with the same attention mechanisms originally designed for text.

definitions.definition: A small, non-overlapping square region of an image, used as the fundamental unit of input for modern vision architectures.
A dense vector representation of an image that captures its high-level semantic meaning in a continuous vector space.
An architecture that applies the transformer model natively to computer vision by treating image patches as sequential tokens.

examples: Explain how an image of a dog chasing a ball is cut into a 16x16 grid of patches, and how the model uses attention to link the patch containing the 'dog' to the patch containing the 'ball'.

relations.builds_toward: ch14 (multimodal data, where image embeddings and text embeddings are combined)

exclusions: Do not explain the mathematical details of self-attention or the transformer equation.
Do not discuss multimodal alignment (like CLIP) here; save it for chapter 14.
